documents.title: NIST Cybersecurity Framework — Overview
documents.extracted_text:
NIST Cybersecurity Framework Overview

The Framework provides a common language for managing cybersecurity risk. It consists of five core functions: Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover. Together these functions provide a high-level strategic view of the lifecycle of an organization's management of cybersecurity risk.

IDENTIFY: Develop organizational understanding to manage cybersecurity risk to systems, assets, data, and capabilities. The activities in this function are foundational for effective use of the Framework. Examples: Asset Management, Business Environment, Governance, Risk Assessment.

PROTECT: Develop and implement appropriate safeguards to ensure delivery of critical services. The Protect function supports the ability to limit or contain the impact of a potential cybersecurity event. Examples: Identity Management, Awareness and Training, Data Security, Information Protection Processes.

DETECT: Develop and implement appropriate activities to identify the occurrence of a cybersecurity event. Examples: Anomalies and Events, Security Continuous Monitoring, Detection Processes.

RESPOND: Develop and implement appropriate activities regarding a detected cybersecurity event. Examples: Response Planning, Communications, Analysis, Mitigation, Improvements.

RECOVER: Develop and implement appropriate activities to maintain plans for resilience and to restore any capabilities or services that were impaired due to a cybersecurity event. Examples: Recovery Planning, Improvements, Communications.

documents.title: Q4 Product Roadmap Brief
documents.extracted_text:
Q4 Product Roadmap Brief

Goals

This quarter the team is focused on three deliverables: improved transcription accuracy on industry jargon, GPU acceleration for faster turnaround, and the browser extension launch.

Transcription Accuracy

The primary success metric is reducing acronym misrecognition rate (e.g., KPI, QBR, SaaS, ARR) below 5%. Approach: expand the bundled vocabulary corpus to include healthcare, legal, finance, and engineering terms. Ship int8-quantized embeddings to keep the corpus under 700 MB.

GPU Acceleration

Target Apple Silicon (already shipping via mlx-whisper) and Windows (CUDA via faster-whisper). Goal: 3x throughput improvement on a 30-minute recording.

Browser Extension

Launch on the Chrome Web Store by end of Q4. Features: capture audio from any tab, screen recording, side-panel chat with the AI assistant, and one-click upload to the local Verbatim app.

Timeline

Week 1-2: vocabulary corpus expansion and int8 quantization
Week 3-4: Windows GPU code path and testing
Week 5-6: Browser extension polish and Web Store submission
Week 7-8: Buffer and post-launch fixes

Risks

The Windows GPU path is the highest-risk item. CUDA driver mismatches and varying consumer GPU configurations may push the schedule. We have buffer in weeks 7-8 to absorb up to a week of slippage.

documents.title: User research interview notes
documents.extracted_text:
User Research — Interview Notes

Participant: P03 — Senior PM at fintech startup
Date: October 2026
Format: 30-min remote interview

Key findings:

1. Privacy is the primary purchase motivator. P03 ruled out cloud-only transcription tools. Their CFO transcripts can't leave the laptop.

2. Existing tools fail on domain vocabulary. P03 cited consistent misrecognition of company-specific acronyms — they switch to manual editing 5-10 times per transcript.

3. Project-based organization is critical. P03 manages 8 ongoing client engagements; needs each transcript / document / note tied to a specific client folder.

4. Voice search > keyboard search. P03 prefers asking the assistant a question over crafting search queries. "What did Sarah say about the pricing model?" feels more natural than "sarah pricing model."

Action items:
- Verify our vocabulary correction handles their acronym list
- Confirm project isolation is intuitive in onboarding
- Test voice chat on multi-speaker transcripts

notes.content: This is exactly what the custom-vocabulary system addresses. Worth checking in with each customer about the specific terms in their domain — the Extract Vocabulary feature in the document toolbar can do this automatically from any uploaded document.
Verbatim's privacy-first architecture maps cleanly to the PROTECT function — local-only data, no cloud round-trip, per-user encryption.
OCR test sample. Run OCR from the document toolbar after downloading a vision model in Settings → AI.

chats.title: Roadmap brief — what are the action items?

messages.content: Can you summarize the action items from the Q4 roadmap brief?

From the Q4 Product Roadmap Brief, the action items are:

1. **Transcription accuracy** — Expand the bundled vocabulary corpus to include healthcare, legal, finance, and engineering terms. Ship int8-quantized embeddings to keep corpus under 700 MB. Target: reduce acronym misrecognition below 5%.

2. **GPU acceleration** — Target Apple Silicon (already shipping via mlx-whisper) and Windows (CUDA via faster-whisper). Goal: 3x throughput improvement on a 30-minute recording.

3. **Browser extension** — Launch on Chrome Web Store by end of Q4. Features include audio capture from any tab, screen recording, side-panel chat, and one-click upload.

**Timeline**: 8 weeks total. Weeks 1-2 are vocabulary work, 3-4 are Windows GPU, 5-6 are browser extension, and 7-8 are buffer + post-launch fixes.
What's the highest-risk item in that timeline?

Based on the brief and typical execution risk, the **Windows GPU acceleration** in weeks 3-4 carries the highest risk:

- **Platform-specific code path** — CUDA via faster-whisper is more brittle than the mlx-whisper Apple Silicon path. Driver mismatches, CUDA version compatibility, and varying Windows GPU configs (NVIDIA vs Intel Arc vs AMD) all introduce variability.
- **Performance target is aggressive** — 3x throughput on a 30-minute recording requires careful batch sizing and may hit memory constraints on consumer GPUs.
- **The brief allows for slippage** — weeks 7-8 are buffer; up to a week of slippage on GPU work is absorbable, but anything beyond that pushes browser-extension launch.

One mitigation worth considering: kick off browser-extension work in parallel with GPU testing (week 3) since they're independent tracks.

chats.title: What did P03 say about vocabulary issues?

messages.content: What did P03 say about vocabulary issues with their current transcription tool?

From the P03 interview notes:

P03 cited **consistent misrecognition of company-specific acronyms** as a major pain point. They reported having to switch to manual editing **5-10 times per transcript** because of these errors.

This aligns directly with finding #2 in the interview: existing tools fail on domain vocabulary. The action item linked to this finding is: "Verify our vocabulary correction handles their acronym list."

Would you like me to draft a follow-up question to send to P03 asking for a sample of the acronyms that consistently fail?
